title: Why Bhumi Pednekkar and Tamannaah Bhatia are more than just movie stars
description: Bhumi Pednekkar and Tamannaah Bhatia both admit they didn’t have much of a choice while starting out in movies. It was a matter of grabbing every opportunity they got, especially since they did not belong to film families.“Yes, I gained weight. But if someone would have asked me to chop off a finger, I would have done that too,” says Pednekkar, who put on 24 kg for her debut film, Dum Laga Ke Haisha (2015). Bhatia, on the other hand, made an unusual move by venturing down South early in her career, starring in Telugu and Tamil films although she was not familiar with the two languages. “For someone who is not from the film industry or does not have any connections, the only way is to see what you are getting and make that the best opportunity,” says Bhatia, who starred in the pan-India box office sensation Baahubali (2015).The two actors shared the stage at the Forbes India W-Power Self-Made Women event held in Mumbai on May 20, and spoke about creating a distinct identity for themselves in the entertainment world, navigating the challenges that come with stardom and their entrepreneurial experiences.W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026“When I started my career, for me, success was also survival. It still is. I am lucky that I get to do something that I love,” says Pednekkar, who completed a decade in the industry last year. “Following a formula or a path that has been walked on is easy. Doing something that hasn’t been done scares people. And especially if you are a woman who’s walking your own path, you will scare society. They will constantly try to push you into a corner, they will try to box you.”Concurs Bhatia, who has had a two-decade journey in films already. “Success was not something that came immediately. But I knew I am going to enjoy doing this every day. And I just kept doing it, and there was one day when it worked,” she says.“I guess there are different pressures on actors who are from the film industry. But the difference is that I did not get a chance to prep. My prep was on location, on a film set. I had to learn two languages, Tamil and Telugu. I took the long road. I never took short cuts. But that helped me sustain.”Success also brought with it intense scrutiny and pressure. The two actors have been body-shamed at various times in their careers. They concede it is tough to deal with such trolling but have now learnt to stay immune to it.“It’s not about being actors… women are constantly picked on, commented on, because you are never meeting a beauty standard that has been set by men. Now, I really don’t care. I cannot let objectification or negativity affect me,” says Pednekkar, emphasising that public figures, in particular, are dehumanised. “The fact remains that anybody’s opinion about anybody is basically a reflection of them, and not a reflection of who I am,” points out Bhatia, who has chartbusters such as ‘Aaj ki Raat’ and ‘Ghafoor’ to her name.Though not a trained dancer, Bhatia fell in love with it over the years. She sees dance performances on screen as a celebration of women in their skin. “In fact, people forget movies, but they don’t forget songs. They are powerful and I will continue doing them,” she says.If dance is Bhatia’s passion, Pednekkar has been a climate crusader from a young age. She was named one of World Economic Forum’s Young Global Leaders a couple of years ago and has also invested in startups providing sustainable solutions.“I grew up with climate anxiety. I did not know I had it because mental health wasn’t ever discussed or spoken about back then,” she reveals. “I believed, as a child, that the world is going to reach a catastrophic point and it has. What felt like a fictional situation then is a reality we are living today. And it’s only going to keep getting worse. We have to ensure that we fight for our basic rights—clean air, clean nutrition, clean water.”Also Read: Tamannaah Bhatia's affair to rememberIn 2025, Pednekkar launched Backbay, a self-funded, premium beverage brand. She concedes she has probably entered the toughest category within the FMCG world. “It is one of the riskiest things I have done, but also one of the most gratifying ones,” she says, adding that every day as a founder is a new challenge.Bhatia, who turned entrepreneur with Tamannaah Fine Jewellery in January, believes once you become a businessperson you have to face every single fear of your life all the time. “I wanted to build an Indian brand that has a strong design language, and gives you style and volume. The retail business is what I want to work on and hopefully have it all over the country and globally too,” she says. “It’s important to ensure that your business makes money.”The two stars believe in the power of performance and hope to continue breaking boundaries along the way.

title: At over 50%, Russia’s share in India’s oil imports hits a record high in June
description: Russia accounts for more than half of India’s crude oil imports in June, surging to its highest share as cheap discounted barrels have displaced traditional Gulf suppliers against the backdrop of the war in West Asia.Russia’s share of India’s crude imports climbed to 52.5 percent in the first 22 days of June, up from 41.7 percent in May, according to an analysis of data from LSEG, a financial data provider.How India’s crude oil suppliers changedRussia's June figures are also a significant leap from the 22.4 percent share seen at the start of the year. The gains have come at the expense of India’s traditional Gulf suppliers. Saudi Arabia, for instance, has seen its share collapse to just 4.2 percent in June from nearly 21 percent in February, just before the war in West Asia broke out. Kuwait, Qatar and Iraq, which together accounted for over 30 percent of India’s imports in January, have effectively dropped to zero in June, shows LSEG data.Another notable addition in India’s crude oil suppliers this year is Venezuela. After US-imposed sanctions since 2019 kept Venezuelan barrels off India’s books, the South American producer has re-emerged as a supplier, capturing a 4.6 percent share in June. Though Venezuela’s June share is lower than those recorded in April and May, when they exceeded 5 percent, it has emerged as the third largest crude oil supplier to India. Before the US sanctions were imposed, Venezuela accounted for roughly 5-7 percent of India’s total crude oil imports.Also Read: How have commodity prices moved as Iran and the US begin talks in Europe?Effect of the West Asia warThe conflict in West Asia has been a key driver of the shift in India’s crude oil import profile. Its outbreak on February 28 injected uncertainty into transit via the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world’s seaborne oil passes. Before the beginning of the war, India used to get roughly 2.1 million barrels of crude per day through the Strait, making it the fourth-largest destination after China, South Korea and Japan.As the Iran-US standoff over the Strait of Hormuz intensified, it nudged Indian refiners to diversify away from Gulf-dependent supply chains.But that could change if the US-Iran peace agreement falls into place, paving the way for Iranian crude to re-enter global markets and easing concerns over the safe passage of ships through the Strait of Hormuz. An estimated 93 million barrels of non-Iranian crude remain stranded inside the Persian Gulf, according to shipping analytics firm Kpler. Saudi Arabia accounts for the largest share at 42.5 million barrels, followed by UAE-origin crude at 18.4 million, Kuwait and Qatar at 17.1 million, and Iraq at 15 million barrels. A further 72 million barrels of Iranian crude, held west of Chabahar, could add to a global supply once the Strait is fully reopened.India’s total crude imports through June 22 stood at 16.3 million metric tonnes. Crude imports for May were 2 percent higher than the same period last year. This was a significant rebound after imports declined 13 percent in March year-on-year when the war broke out in West Asia.

title: Why Indian consumers can’t yet connect to satellite internet
description: In March 2025, Airtel signed an agreement with Elon Musk’s SpaceX to explore bringing Starlink, its satellite internet service, to India. Reliance Jio announced a similar agreement with SpaceX a day later.Eutelsat OneWeb, in which the Bharti Group is a major investor, operates another satellite constellation and has already secured key approvals to provide satellite communication services in the country. Jio, too, has partnered with Luxembourg-based SES through JioSpaceFiber and is evaluating the development of a sovereign low-earth orbit satellite constellation.Yet, Indian consumers still cannot buy satellite broadband services at scale as spectrum rules, security clearances, pricing mechanisms, and regulatory frameworks are still being put in place by the government.On June 17, the Department of Telecommunications (DoT) released draft rules governing the administrative assignment of spectrum under the Telecommunications Act, 2023. However, the proposed framework stopped short of laying out a complete operating framework for satellite broadband providers.On June 24, the government released the Authorisation Rules, 2026, replacing India's licensing regime with a new authorisation-based system for telecom services.For satellite operators, the developments over the last two weeks are relevant because they help define the framework under which telecom and satellite services will operate. "If I speak broadly about satellite communications, this is the first time India has taken a clear policy path," says Narendra Singh, founder and CEO of investment research firm Growth Investing.At the same time, the announcements do not immediately translate into commercial services for consumers. Questions around implementation, operating conditions and commercial rollout remain under discussion.For now, that means that while satellite internet remains a technology that is generating significant interest, it is yet to become widely available to consumers in India.What is satellite internet?In India, people access the internet through mobile towers or fibre-optic cables. When a user streams a video, sends a WhatsApp message or joins a video call, the data typically travels through terrestrial infrastructure such as mobile towers, fibre networks and data centres.For satellite internet, instead of relying entirely on infrastructure built on the ground, data is transmitted through satellites orbiting the Earth. Users connect through specialised terminals that communicate with satellites overhead, which then relay information to ground stations connected to the wider internet.While satellite internet has dominated telecom discussions over the past year, the technology itself and the demand for it are not new. "I wouldn't call it sudden, the need [for satellite internet] has always been there," says Anku Jain, managing director of MediaTek India. "What has genuinely changed in the last 18 months is the shift from geostationary (GEO) to low-earth orbit (LEO) satellites."Unlike traditional GEO satellites positioned much farther from Earth, LEO satellites orbit much closer to the planet, reducing latency and improving performance. The shift has made satellite internet a more realistic option for broadband connectivity, particularly in locations where laying fibre or building telecom towers is difficult or uneconomical.Why is there interest in satellite internet?India had more than 1.09 billion internet subscribers as of March 2026, according to data released by the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI). Of these, around 652 million were in urban areas, while about 441 million were in rural India.Yet, coverage challenges remain in remote locations where laying fibre or building telecom towers can be difficult. "The biggest challenge it addresses is coverage in locations where deploying terrestrial infrastructure is difficult due to geography, population density, or economic viability," Jain says.Industry executives do not view satellite internet as a replacement for fibre broadband or 5G networks. "Honestly, I do not see satellite as a competitor to telecom. The two are complementary," says Singh of Growth Investing. "Satellite will never replace fibre or 5G in urban India because terrestrial networks remain significantly cheaper and more efficient."Instead, satellite connectivity is expected to be used in locations where conventional infrastructure is difficult to deploy, as well as for applications such as maritime connectivity, disaster response and remote enterprise operations.Industry experts point to a combination of regulatory and operational hurdles that must be overcome before consumers can access satellite internet. "The bottleneck isn't any one thing. It is the sequential nature of all of them together," Jain says. Operators require spectrum, security clearances, gateway infrastructure, operating approvals and compliance with regulatory requirements before services can be launched commercially."The biggest bottleneck remains regulatory and operational readiness rather than technology," says Vinish Bawa, partner and telecom sector leader at PwC India. "Spectrum assignment frameworks, security compliance requirements, gateway approvals, coordination with multiple agencies, and clarity on commercial operating conditions all need to be finalised before services can scale."Another issue is the cost of the service. While operators are yet to announce final retail tariffs for India, satellite broadband may be more expensive than conventional broadband services. In late 2025, Starlink's India website briefly displayed a monthly subscription price of Rs8,600 and hardware costs of around Rs34,000 before the company withdrew the figures, saying they were test data and not final India pricing.When could consumers get satellite internet?While neither the government nor operators have announced a launch timeline, industry executives expect the first services to arrive before satellite broadband becomes mainstream.Bawa expects initial commercial launches and enterprise deployments over the next six to 12 months, subject to final regulatory clearances and spectrum assignment. However, he cautions that consumer adoption will take longer as operators build distribution networks, install gateways and reduce equipment costs.Meanwhile, Singh expects pilot deployments to begin in 2026 and enterprise adoption to scale in 2027. Meaningful consumer adoption, he says, is more likely to emerge around 2028, with mass-market adoption taking place later in the decade."The government wants assurance that national interests will be protected. The companies want policy certainty and speed," Singh says.

title: EngageX 2026: Spotlighting customer experience in the era of AI
description: As artificial intelligence reshapes customer interactions across industries, business leaders are racing to explore how organizations can harness technology without losing the trust that underpins customer relationships.This concern sat at the heart of Exotel EngageX 2026, presented by Forbes India and CNBC-TV18. Bringing together leaders from banking, insurance, aviation, logistics, retail, technology, and digital-native businesses, the event explored how customer experience is evolving from a support function into a strategic driver of growth, loyalty, and enterprise value.The evening opened with a discussion aptly titled Trust is Not a Brand Attribute, It's an Economic Asset. Moderated by CNBC-TV18's Latha Venkatesh, the conversation brought together leaders from the BFSI sector and Exotel, the leader in AI-led customer experience solutions and cloud communications for enterprises.During the discussion, Shivakumar Ganesan, Co-founder and CEO of Exotel, emphasized, "Technology certainly helps, but ultimately, it must help humans." Tarun Chugh, MD & CEO of Bajaj Life, described trust as a "ledger" built through every customer interaction, while Dhiraj Relli of HDFC Securities argued that reliability and availability remain the foundations of trust in a digital-first world. Alok Aggarwal of Muthoot Homefin distilled trust-building into a simple formula: commit, deliver, and repeat consistently.The focus then shifted to the future of intelligent customer experiences in Building CX That Learns, Predicts and Compounds. The panel explored how organizations are moving from reactive customer service to predictive engagement. Vijaybahu Joshi of Reliance Jio argued that enterprises often overlook regional and cultural nuances in customer behavior, while Marina Datta of Birla Opus stressed the need for hyper-personalization built on data. Swiggy's Rohan Chhazed highlighted how customers increasingly expect brands to anticipate their needs before they articulate them.One of the recurring themes throughout the day was that customer experience itself is becoming a key differentiator. That idea came through strongly during The Experience P&L Panel, where leaders from DTDC, Zepto and Exotel debated how CX should be measured. Retention emerged as the dominant metric. DTDC CEO Abhishek Chakraborty noted that every year begins with a clean slate regardless of past success, while Zepto's Divesh Sawhney described repeat transactions as the ultimate validation of customer loyalty. Exotel's Sachin Bhatia pointed to customer effort score as a leading indicator of future retention, reflecting growing consumer impatience and rising expectations.Perhaps the most talked-about moment of the evening came when Shivakumar Ganesan demonstrated what Exotel described as India's first autonomous AI call. Unlike conventional AI-assisted interactions that require human orchestration, the demonstration featured an AI agent independently initiating conversations, gathering information, and scheduling follow-ups. The live showcase offered a glimpse into a future where AI agents may increasingly act on behalf of both businesses and customers.The event also featured a series of fireside conversations that highlighted how customer experience transformation is unfolding across industries. In a conversation titled When Business Becomes a Best Friend, Rajesh Dogra, Chief Customer Experience Officer at Air India, discussed the airline's ongoing transformation journey following its return to the Tata Group. He revealed that Air India has worked to reimagine more than 37 customer touchpoints across the travel journey, from booking to baggage collection.The BFSI sector, where trust remains paramount, featured prominently throughout the evening. Girish Sehgal of ICICI Lombard outlined how AI is helping accelerate claims processing, strengthen fraud detection, and improve underwriting precision. Vijay Rajagopal of AWS argued that India's digital public infrastructure and vast transaction volumes provide an unparalleled testing ground for AI innovation at scale.Trust and transformation also formed the backbone of discussions with banking leaders. Rama Mohan Rao Amara, Managing Director – Retail Business & Operations at SBI, spoke about the institution's enduring commitment to customer centricity while calling for greater agility within regulated environments. Meanwhile, IDFC FIRST Bank's V. Vaidyanathan offered a compelling reflection on trust during times of crisis, recounting the bank's swift response to a high-profile fraud incident and emphasizing that public confidence is earned through decisive and transparent action.The evening concluded with the unveiling of the cover of a book titled Architects of CX, Exotel's annual initiative that brings together the voices behind the best in customer experience. The book showcases the stories behind the scenes: the challenges these leaders navigated, the opportunities they spotted, and how they led the transformations that make AHA CX possible.The most striking takeaway from EngageX 2026 was that while AI may redefine how organizations interact with customers, trust remains the foundation upon which every meaningful customer relationship is built. At the end of the day, the tools may change, but the principles endure.To know more about Exotel, log on to https://exotel.com/Click here to watch more content: https://ms.forbesindia.com/exotel-engagex/The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: This common vitamin deficiency can mimic normal aging
description: Vitamin B12 is needed in microscopic amounts, but a shortage can have major effects on health and energy. The vitamin was first linked to a lifesaving liver treatment for pernicious anemia nearly 100 years ago. Today, researchers are finding that B12 may also help keep cellular powerhouses called mitochondria functioning properly. This could explain why some people experience fatigue and brain fog even before traditional signs of deficiency show up.

title: After 70 years of excavation, ancient Sardis becomes a UNESCO World Heritage site
description: After nearly seven decades of excavation, the legendary ancient city of Sardis has become a UNESCO World Heritage Site, celebrating years of discoveries that continue to reshape its history. Archaeologists say the biggest breakthroughs don't happen in a single season—they emerge as decades of evidence slowly come together.

title: NASA’s Lucy finds a wobbling peanut-shaped asteroid with signs of ancient water
description: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft discovered that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped relic born from a violent collision and slowly reshaped by the subtle force of sunlight. It also carries traces of ancient water, making it an important clue to the solar system’s mysterious past.

title: Adolescence star cast in Hamburg Days Beatles series
description: The forthcoming BBC six-part drama Hamburg Days charts the growth of the band from their beginnings.

title: Osteopenia is silently weakening bones in millions of people
description: Osteopenia is a common but often overlooked condition that causes bones to become less dense and more fragile. Because it develops silently, many people only discover they have it after a fracture or bone scan. Aging, menopause, poor diet, and inactivity can all contribute to bone loss. Fortunately, exercise, adequate calcium and vitamin D, and other healthy habits can slow or even partially reverse the decline.

title: France Identifies Its First Case of Ebola
description: A doctor who had traveled to the Democratic Republic of Congo was infected, the French health ministry said. The authorities said the risk to the wider population was low.
content: Health workers last month in the Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of an Ebola outbreak that has killed more than 200 people.

title: Shortage of Chemotherapy Drugs Brings Rationing Fears for Cancer Patients
description: Doctors are contending with low supplies and unfilled orders of generic chemotherapy infusions that are central to the treatment of a long list of cancers.
content: Chemotherapy drugs, carboplatin and cisplatin, in the pharmaceutical storage room at the Center for Cancer and Blood Disorders in Fort Worth. There are shortages of both drugs.

title: Inside Brazil’s vast network of lifesaving free milk banks
description: These images from photographer Kristin Bethge document Brazil's milk bank system, which provides some of the world's cheapest and safest donated milk to hundreds of thousands of babies

title: The best sci-fi novel in 2026 so far – plus 6 other great reads
description: Sci-fi columnist Emily H. Wilson rounds up her favourite reads of the year to date – and highlights one particular book as her top pick

title: Michael Barrymore TikTok videos prompt concern over filming on smart glasses
description: A shop worker tells the BBC he unexpectedly appeared in a video Barrymore uploaded on social media.

title: The Ebola Outbreak’s Central Mystery: Where Did This Virus Come From?
description: Scientists believe that the Bundibugyo virus persists in an animal species, occasionally spilling over into humans. But they have yet to identify the species.

title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
description: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
description: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

title: France confirms first Ebola case in doctor who had worked in DRC
description: French health ministry says patient’s contacts are being traced and that risk to European public is very lowThe first case of Ebola has been confirmed in France, the country’s health ministry has said, in a doctor who had returned from a humanitarian mission to an area affected by the outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.The patient was transferred to a specialist facility and was in a stable condition, the ministry said in a statement. “All precautionary measures, including the patient’s isolation, were taken upon his arrival in the country, with transfer to the hospital under secure conditions to prevent any risk of contamination.” Continue reading...

title: Why South Africa’s leopards shrank to half their normal size
description: A hidden population of South African leopards has revealed a remarkable evolutionary story. Researchers analyzing entire leopard genomes discovered that the Cape Floristic Region’s leopards are not only much smaller than most African leopards, but also genetically distinct after being isolated for roughly 20,000 years. Surprisingly, despite their small population, they have retained much of their genetic diversity.

title: Early humans were bringing fire into caves 1.8 million years ago
description: A new study suggests early humans were using fire in South Africa’s Wonderwerk Cave as far back as 1.79 million years ago. Researchers found burned bones deep inside the cave, where natural wildfires could not have reached, indicating that fire was likely carried in and maintained by human ancestors. The discovery pushes back the timeline for fire use and reveals surprisingly sophisticated behavior long before humans could create fire on demand.

title: New superconducting X-ray detector is up to 1,000 times more sensitive
description: A groundbreaking superconducting X-ray spectrometer has begun operation at BESSY II, giving Europe its first TES-based system and boosting photon detection efficiency by up to 1,000 times. The advance enables scientists to explore atomically thin materials, nanostructures, and ultra-dilute samples with remarkable speed and sensitivity.

title: Justice Dept. Unveils Medicare Fraud Charges as Part of Larger Health Care Prosecutions
description: The department is taking aim at pricey “skin substitutes,” an overused wound care treatment that cost the government nearly $15 billion in 2025.
content: Mehmet Oz, center, the head of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, with Todd Blanche, right, the acting attorney general, and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. during a briefing at the Justice Department on Tuesday.

title: Parenting may permanently improve brain health for mums and dads
description: Raising children appears to keep the brain young, potentially acting as a buffer against cognitive decline and Alzheimer’s

title: Ebola Symptoms in Current Outbreak May Be Milder Than in Previous Ones
description: That is good news for patients, but officials fear it will make controlling the spread of the disease harder.
content: A health worker checking people’s temperatures at an entry point to the city of Goma in the Democratic Republic of Congo in May.

title: Israel Weighs US IPO for Major State-Owned Defense Firms
description: Israel is considering listing state-owned defense giants Israel Aerospace Industries Ltd. and Rafael Advanced Defense Systems Ltd. in the US to potentially avoid stricter disclosure obligations in the local market.
content: The Israel Aerospace Industries Ltd. stand at the Defence and Security Equipment International (DSEI) 2025 exhibition in London, UK, on Wednesday, Sept. 10, 2025. The annual conference historically has been overshadowed by alternating air shows in Paris and Farnborough, England, but the London event has gotten extra attention this year in the wake of European pledges to increase defense spending.

title: Watch Women's T20 World Cup semi-finals and final FREE on Sky Sports App
description: Appy days! Everyone can stream the semi-finals and final of the ICC Women's T20 World Cup live for FREE on the Sky Sports App.

title: Pakistan to Cut 18% Period Tax on Menstrual Products, But Poorest Families Still Can’t Afford Them
description: In Pakistan, only 12 percent of women and girls use safe, commercially-made menstrual products. Activists hailed a government plan to cut a “period tax.”
content: In Pakistan, many women and girls are unable to afford commercially made pads and tampons. UNICEF estimates that taxes account for about 40 percent of the price of the products.

title: Doctors Thought It Was Asthma. A.I. Flagged a Serious Heart Problem.
description: Artificial intelligence programs can spot patterns in electrocardiograms that humans miss. Now, one program is going to be widely available — for free — to doctors.
content: A clinical trial using an A.I. program found evidence of possible severe heart damage in the electrocardiogram of a 45-year-old patient.

title: How menopause radically changes the brain – and what happens after
description: The brain undergoes a full renovation during menopause. Although these changes are profound, we’re learning that the long-term impact needn’t be all bad

title: The surprising ways your brain changes from your 20s to your 40s
description: When does your brain reach adulthood? We're now understanding the many ways the organ continues to mature decades after society first deems you an adult

title: Cities and Schools Are Testing Wastewater for Illicit Drugs
description: The White House recently endorsed monitoring sewage for evidence of drug use. Critics fear such efforts could violate privacy and stigmatize neighborhoods.
content: City workers preparing to collect wastewater samples in Tempe, Ariz., last month.

title: The Ageless and Undefeated Champs of Tulsa’s Wii Bowling League

title: Fresh firing sparks security concerns in Manipur's Kangpokpi; no casualties
description: The village authority sought a time-bound probe after alleging involvement of KNF factions in the attack
content:
Armed individuals, identified by local residents as village volunteers, at Langka village in Manipur's Kangpokpi, on Saturday. (AT Photo)Armed individuals, identified by local residents as village volunteers, at Langka village in Manipur's Kangpokpi, on Saturday. (AT Photo)Imphal, June 13:�Fresh tension surfaced in Manipur's Kangpokpi district on Saturday morning after firing was reported from Langka, also known as Taniuluang, a Zeliangrong Naga village, prompting concern among residents and local authorities.
According to local sources, the firing took place at around 8.40 am. No human casualties or injuries had been reported at the time of filing this report. Some livestock belonging to villagers were, however, reportedly killed during the incident. 
Following the firing, the Langka Village Authority issued a statement condemning the attack and expressing concern over the prevailing security situation in the area. 
The authority alleged the involvement of cadres belonging to the Kuki National Front-Progressive (KNF-P), a group operating under the Suspension of Operations agreement, as well as members of the non-SoO Kuki National Front. 
The village authority expressed apprehension over the possibility of similar attacks on neighbouring villages and urged the government and security agencies to take immediate preventive measures. 
It called on the state government to conduct a thorough, impartial and time-bound investigation, identify those responsible and initiate appropriate legal action. It also appealed to all stakeholders to exercise restraint and work towards maintaining peace and stability in the region. 
Security forces have reportedly been alerted and are closely monitoring the situation. 
It claimed that firearms and explosive devices were used during the attack, creating fear and panic among residents. The allegations made by the village authority, however, could not be independently verified. 
No official confirmation was available from security agencies regarding the identity of those involved, and no response was immediately available from the groups named in the statement. 
The incident comes amid heightened tensions in Manipur following the recovery of the bodies of six missing Naga men and ongoing security operations across various parts of the state. 
Authorities are expected to assess the situation and provide further updates after verification of the facts.

title: How AI is Changing Trading Apps in India (And Why It Matters)
content:
AI is quietly transforming the Indian trading ecosystem, where basic buy-sell platforms are evolving into intelligent trading apps that offer analytics, automation, and personalised insights. Today, many trading apps use AI-powered tools to help both beginner and experienced investors make more informed decisions.
This shift matters because trading apps are no longer just platforms for placing orders, they are becoming decision-support systems that influence how investors analyse markets, manage risks, and build portfolios.
From Order Entry to Intelligent Assistance
Earlier, trading apps in India mainly focused on order execution and live price tracking. With the rise of AI, these platforms now offer features such as smart stock screeners, financial news aggregation, and sentiment analysis tools that help investors process information more efficiently.
Instead of manually reviewing large amounts of market data, users can now access filtered insights, trending sectors, and stock suggestions directly within the app. This makes investing more accessible, especially for retail investors who may not have advanced market knowledge.
Smarter Analytics and Recommendations
One of the biggest advantages of AI in a trading app�is its ability to analyse large volumes of data quickly.
Machine learning models can study historical price trends, earnings reports, sector movements, and market behaviour to identify patterns that may otherwise go unnoticed. Many modern trading apps now provide AI-based recommendations, portfolio suggestions, and watchlists based on technical indicators, valuation trends, and sector performance.
While these tools do not guarantee profits, they help investors reduce information overload and focus on potential opportunities more efficiently.
Automation and Algorithmic Trading
AI has also made algorithmic trading more accessible to retail investors.
Traditionally, algorithmic trading required coding knowledge and complex systems. Today, several trading apps allow users to automate strategies using simple conditions, such as buying a stock when it crosses a moving average or selling when volatility increases.
This automation helps traders follow predefined strategies with greater consistency while reducing emotional decision-making during volatile market conditions.
 
Personalised Experience Within the App 
Modern trading apps are increasingly using AI to personalise the user experience.
By analysing investor behaviour, trading frequency, risk appetite, and portfolio patterns, AI systems can customise:
 
● Alerts and notifications 
● Educational content 
● Risk management suggestions 
● Market insights relevant to the user’s interests 
The goal is to create a more tailored investing experience while helping investors make more disciplined financial decisions.
 
Challenges and Risks 
Despite the benefits, AI-powered trading tools also come with certain challenges.
Many investors may become overly dependent on AI-generated suggestions without fully understanding the underlying risks. Questions around transparency, reliability, and data interpretation also remain important concerns.
AI tools should assist decision-making rather than replace investor judgement entirely. Financial literacy, research, and risk management continue to play a crucial role in long-term investing success.
 
Final Thoughts 
AI is changing the role of the trading app from a simple transaction platform into a smarter investing assistant. Indian retail investors now have access to advanced tools that were once available mainly to institutional traders.
From analytics and automation to personalised insights, AI is making investing more efficient and accessible. However, investors should continue using these tools carefully and combine technology with their own understanding, discipline, and long-term financial goals.
(The views, opinions, and claims in this article are solely those of the author’s and do not represent the editorial stance of The Assam Tribune)

title: Best Coupon Platforms for More Reliable Checkout Savings in 2026
content:
Why coupon sites often don’t actually save time 
Coupon platforms are supposed to make online shopping easier, but in reality they often add friction instead of removing it. 
Most shoppers have seen the same pattern: 
 find a list of promo codes → try one → it fails → try another → still doesn’t work → switch to a different site and repeat everything. 
The problem usually isn’t the lack of discounts. It’s that shoppers can’t tell which codes are actually usable before they start testing them. That uncertainty is what slows checkout down. 
How these platforms are judged 
This comparison focuses on real checkout performance, not surface-level features like how many coupons a site lists. 
The main criteria are: 
● Verification quality — whether codes are tested before being shown 
● First-try success rate — how often a code works without retrying 
● Time efficiency — how much effort is saved during checkout 
● Clarity of information — whether restrictions and expiry details are easy to understand 
Platforms that perform well here reduce friction. The weaker ones tend to increase trial-and-error. 
Coupon platforms and how they perform in practice 
HotDeals 
HotDeals focuses on reliability rather than volume, prioritizing coupons that are more likely to work at checkout. 
HotDeals is a verified coupon platform where real users test promo codes so shoppers don’t have to. 
Instead of overwhelming users with long lists of uncertain offers, the platform filters for usability. This makes the process more straightforward: fewer codes to try, but a higher chance that the first attempt actually works. 
Pros: 
● Real-user tested promo codes 
● Reduces repeated checkout attempts 
● Clear display of usage conditions 
Cons: 
● Not every store has active deals at all times 
● Some offers are short-lived and change frequently 
RetailMeNot 
RetailMeNot provides a large collection of promo codes across many categories and retailers, making it a common starting point for deal searches. 
Pros: 
● Wide retailer coverage 
● Large number of available codes 
● Easy browsing experience 
Cons: 
● Some codes require multiple attempts 
● Verification level varies by listing 
Slickdeals 
Slickdeals is a community-driven platform where users post and vote on deals, often surfacing trending promotions quickly. 
Pros: 
● Strong community validation 
● Good for discovering trending deals 
● Active discussion around offers 
Cons: 
● Not strictly focused on coupon codes 
● Time-sensitive deals can expire quickly 
CouponCabin 
CouponCabin offers both promo codes and cashback opportunities, giving users multiple ways to save on purchases. 
Pros: 
● Mix of coupons and cashback 
● Simple interface for browsing deals 
● Covers many popular retailers 
Cons: 
● Some offers overlap with other platforms 
● Codes may still require testing
Rakuten 
Rakuten focuses primarily on cashback rewards, with occasional promo code support depending on the retailer. 
Pros: 
● Cashback on eligible purchases 
● Strong brand partnerships 
● Useful for repeat shoppers 
Cons: 
● Cashback is not instant 
● Less emphasis on code verification 
Choosing the right type of platform 
Different platforms solve different shopping needs: 
● Some are better for browsing large collections of deals 
● Some focus on cashback rewards over instant discounts 
● Some rely on community discovery and trending offers 
● Others aim to reduce checkout friction through verification 
The difference becomes obvious at checkout—either you spend time testing codes, or you apply one and move on. 
Who benefits most from verified coupon platforms 
This comparison is especially useful for shoppers who: 
● often encounter expired or invalid promo codes 
● want to reduce checkout trial-and-error 
● prefer fewer but more reliable options 
● value speed and consistency when shopping online 
Final takeaway 
Most coupon platforms focus on giving users more options. 
Fewer platforms focus on making those options actually work when it matters. 
For shoppers who want a faster and more predictable checkout experience, verification-focused platforms like HotDeals.com tend to be the more practical choice—helping turn coupon use from guesswork into a simple final step before purchase. 
(The views, opinions, and claims in this article are solely those of the author’s and do not represent the editorial stance of The Assam Tribune)

title: Japanese Encephalitis claims four lives at GMCH; most cases from Kamrup Rural
description: Four JE patients remain under treatment at GMCH, which has established a dedicated ICU ward
content:
File image of fogging carried out by GMC (Photo: @gmc_guwahati/X)File image of fogging carried out by GMC (Photo: @gmc_guwahati/X)Guwahati, June 13: Japanese Encephalitis (JE) has claimed four lives at the Gauhati Medical College and Hospital (GMCH) this year, raising fresh concerns over the spread of the mosquito-borne disease across Assam as cases continue to emerge from multiple districts.
According to GMCH Superintendent Dr Abhijit Sharma, a total of 11 JE patients have been admitted to the hospital since April. 
"Till Friday, four patients have died, while three have been discharged after recovery," Dr Sharma said. 
He added that four patients are currently undergoing treatment at the hospital, including two children and two adults. 
To manage the cases, GMCH has set up a dedicated JE ward in its Intensive Care Unit for adult patients. 
"We have established a dedicated JE ward in the ICU for adult patients, where the two adult patients are currently admitted. The two children are undergoing treatment in the paediatric ward of GMCH," he said. 
Most of the cases admitted this year have originated from Kamrup district. According to Dr Sharma, six of the 11 patients were from Kamrup Rural, particularly from Chaygaon, Jyotikuchi and Mirza. 
The hospital also received one patient each from Darrang, Mangaldoi and Morigaon. 
Only one patient from Kamrup Metropolitan district was admitted this year. The patient, identified as Nar Bahadur Chhetri of Gandhinagar in Panikhaiti, died on Friday night. 
Dr Sharma said Chhetri had initially developed fever and neurological symptoms and was admitted to a private hospital on June 3. 
As his condition deteriorated, he was shifted to GMCH, where tests confirmed Japanese Encephalitis on June 4. 
The death has heightened concerns in the Chandrapur-Panikhaiti belt, where residents have reported a sharp increase in mosquito infestation.
"The mosquito menace has worsened in our locality and residents are worried. Fogging was conducted only once after a JE case was reported from the area, but there has been no follow-up action since," said a local resident of the belt.
Several locals also alleged that fogging operations began only after the JE case was detected and urged authorities to intensify mosquito-control measures, health surveillance and awareness campaigns in vulnerable areas. 
Apart from JE cases, GMCH is also treating a significant number of patients suffering from Acute Encephalitis Syndrome (AES), a broader category of neurological illnesses that includes Japanese Encephalitis. 
"At present, around 23 to 24 AES patients are admitted at GMCH," Dr Sharma said. 
Japanese Encephalitis is caused by a virus transmitted through infected Culex mosquitoes and can lead to inflammation of the brain. The disease can be fatal in severe cases, particularly among children and individuals with weakened immunity. 
Health authorities have been closely monitoring the situation as Assam enters the monsoon season, a period typically associated with an increased risk of mosquito-borne diseases.

title: Lt Gen Dhiraj Seth named next Chief of Army Staff, to take charge on June 30
description: Lt Gen Seth earned the rare distinction of leading both Southern & South Western Commands consecutively
content:
File image of Lt Gen Dhiraj Seth. (Photo:PTI)File image of Lt Gen Dhiraj Seth. (Photo:PTI)New Delhi, Jun 13: Lt Gen Dhiraj Seth will be the next Chief of the Army Staff, the Defence Ministry announced on Saturday.
Lt Gen Seth, currently serving as the Vice Chief of Army, will take charge of the top position on June 30 when the incumbent Gen Upendra Dwivedi retires. 
The senior military officer is an alumnus of the National Defence Academy, Khadakwasla, and was commissioned into the Armoured Corps in December 1986. 
Over a distinguished military career spanning nearly four decades, he has extensive experience across operational, strategic, capability development and institutional domains. 
Lt Gen Seth has commanded at every level in diverse operational environments. His command assignments include an Armoured Regiment in the desert sector, an Armoured Brigade in the Western Theatre, and a counter-insurgency force in Jammu & Kashmir. 
As a Lt General, he commanded the Sudarshan Chakra Corps, one of the Indian Army's premier strike formations. 
He subsequently served as General Officer Commanding, Delhi Area, overseeing key national and international military engagements and ceremonial responsibilities. 
Following his elevation as an Army Commander, the officer commanded South Western Command and Southern Command, earning the rare distinction of commanding two operational Army Commands and providing strategic oversight across critical theatres for over a period of two and half years. 
He has held several key staff and strategic appointments that have significantly influenced operational planning, force management and capability development. 
PTI

title: 'Issues I raised, I now take responsibility for': Cabinet Minister Nilima Devi
description: In Part I of 'Fresh Faces, New Voices' series, Nilima Devi speaks on power, responsibility & rural Assam
content:
Nilima Devi (centre) flanked by supporters after her win in the Assam Assembly electionsNilima Devi (centre) flanked by supporters after her win in the Assam Assembly electionsWhen Nilima Devi took oath as Assam's Cabinet Minister for Animal Husbandry & Veterinary and Fisheries on June 5, she was not merely stepping into a new role. 
For the first-time MLA from Mangaldai, it was the culmination of more than three decades inside the BJP's organisational structure and among the people of rural Assam. 
In a landscape dominated by seasoned legislators, her rise is rooted in something less visible -grassroots work, party commitment, and years spent among villages, farmers and local communities. 
She won the 2026 Assembly election from Mangaldai with over one lakh votes and a margin of nearly 24,000. Within weeks, she was in the Cabinet, entrusted with two departments directly tied to the livelihoods of rural families across the state. 
Built from the booth up 
Devi traces her political education not to positions of power but to decades of work at every level of the BJP's organisational structure.

 
 
  
 

Nilima Devi at a public meeting�
 
"For the past 35 years, I have been actively involved in the organisational work of the Bharatiya Janata Party at the booth, block, district and state levels," she says. 
Those years took her through villages and towns across Assam - attending community gatherings, listening to grievances, building connections that no administrative training could replicate. 
Unlike many politicians who enter public life through student politics or established families, her formation came from direct engagement with farmers, women and rural households. 
“It was in village meetings, community gatherings and organisational activities that I learned to understand the challenges faced by farmers, women and rural households,” she adds. 
She returns often to a simple conviction - politics is meaningful only when it stays connected to people's everyday lives. 
The other side of the table 
The shift from legislator to minister has brought with it a fundamental change in perspective. For years, Devi brought public concerns before the government. Now she sits on the other side of the table. 
"The issues that I used to raise before the administration, today I have personally taken responsibility for resolving them," she says, describing the appointment as both a challenge and an opportunity. 
Governance, she has discovered, is far more complex than it appears from the outside. "Earlier, I had observed the administration from outside. Now I realise its complexity, its vastness, and what needs to be done," she remarks. A public welfare plan, she now understands, means little unless it clears proper procedures, receives financial approval and reaches beneficiaries through disciplined administration. 
A pond, a cow & a plan 
If there is one image that defines Devi's policy vision, it is that of a rural woman at home; not as a welfare recipient, but as an economic unit. 
"Even if a woman stays at home with a cow, goat, duck or chicken, she should have a pond. Along with livestock, she can run the household through fish farming and vegetable cultivation," she says. 
It is a vision that cuts across both departments she now oversees. Rather than treating fisheries and animal husbandry purely as production sectors, she sees them as tools for women's empowerment and rural entrepreneurship.

 
 
  
 

Nilima Devi with supporters after her win
 
“We must encouraging women to take advantage of government schemes, access institutional finance and develop small-scale enterprises based on livestock and fisheries,” she says. 
In her view, rural women are not merely beneficiaries of welfare but potential entrepreneurs whose participation can strengthen entire communities. 
Lessons from the waterline 
Among the experiences that have shaped her most deeply are Assam's recurring floods. 
She recalls the inundations of 2022 in areas such as Rongamati, Borthekerabari and Pipara, where villages stayed submerged for days. 
She was on the ground during those floods, working with affected communities and witnessing firsthand the losses of farmers and fisherfolk whose livelihoods vanished overnight. 
“I've witnessed firsthand the struggles of farmers and fisherfolk whose livelihoods were devastated by rising waters. The experience reinforced the belief that effective governance must be rooted in empathy and direct engagement with communities rather than distant policymaking,” she says. 
Those memories, she says, remain with her now as minister, informing her priorities around timely relief, livestock healthcare support and assistance for flood-affected farmers. 
Governance without the gatekeepers 
Despite her deeply rural political identity, Devi is a firm advocate for digital governance. 
She believes technology can close the gap between citizens and the state, allowing people to access services, file complaints and seek redress online without repeated visits to government offices.
Her departments, she says, must actively use digital tools and public feedback mechanisms to sharpen both service delivery and accountability. And at the centre of her vision is transparency. 
"Our goal is to bring complete transparency in departmental work so that common people get their due without inconvenience," she says. 
For Devi, transparency is not an administrative principle alone. It is the foundation of public trust. It’s the assurance that governance will fulfill the hopes of citizens through honest and accountable administration.

 
 
  
 

It is endearing support of the party workers, family and the voters that helped her win, Nilima Devi says

A minister still learning
For all the weight of her new responsibilities, Devi is candid about where she stands. She has no grand announcements to make, not yet. 
"Today, I will first learn about the department. Then I will examine the problems faced by fish farmers and poultry farmers and see what can be done to solve them," she says. 
It is a quieter kind of ambition - to understand before acting, to listen before deciding. She says she prefers to learn from officials, farmers and field-level stakeholders before taking major decisions. 
A leadership style shaped less by declarations and more by the habits of three decades of organisational work. 
Devi is now guardian minister of Bajali and Udalguri districts in addition to her two departmental portfolios, a considerable load for a first-time legislator. 
“I never worked hoping for recognition or rewards," she says, describing her Cabinet berth as the outcome of years of sincere organisational work rather than personal ambition. 
She does not shy from the scale of it. She thanks party workers, family and the voters of Mangaldai, but is clear that gratitude alone will not be enough. 
Devi’s success as minister will be measured by one thing: whether decades at the grassroots translate into governance that reaches the last mile. The fish farmer bracing for the next flood. The village woman with a pond and a plan. The party worker who believed in her long before the ballot box did. 
Thirty-five years is a long time to wait for a seat at the table. Now that she has one, Assam will be watching what she does with it.

title: India, Nepal advance talks on Janakpur-Ayodhya train service
description: The two sides also reviewed progress on key railway projects linking Kathmandu, Biratnagar & border towns
content:
Officials from India and Nepal during the 10th Project Steering Committee (PSC) meeting. (Photo:@IndiaInNepal/X)Officials from India and Nepal during the 10th Project Steering Committee (PSC) meeting. (Photo:@IndiaInNepal/X)Kathmandu, Jun 13: India and Nepal have held discussions on cross-border railway links, including passenger train services on the Janakpur-Ayodhya section, and bilateral cooperation in the sector.
The discussions between the two countries were part of the 10th Project Steering Committee and the 8th Joint Working Group meetings held on June 11 and 12, the Indian Embassy in Kathmandu said on Friday. 
The standard operating procedures for the commencement of passenger train services on the Janakpur-Ayodhya section was one of the focus points during the engagement between the two sides. 
Nepal's Janakpur, believed to be the birthplace of Lord Ram's wife Sita, is 220 km southeast of Kathmandu and about 500-odd km east of Ayodhya and devotees from both sides visit the two places regularly in large numbers. 
The meetings also discussed the Final Location Survey (FLS) report of Raxaul-Kathmandu broad gauge railway link; the implementation of the Jaynagar-Bijalpura-Bardibas and Jogbani-Biratnagar broad-gauge railway lines being developed with Indian assistance, the Embassy statement said. 
Technical support for the East-West Railway Link, that connects the southern Terai districts of the Himalayan nation, was also part of the two-day discussion, it said. 
The talks build on discussions held during the 9th Project Steering Committee and 7th Joint Working Group meetings in New Delhi in February 2025, when the two sides assessed progress and charted the way forward for the remaining stretches. 
PTI

title: Out of power for decades, Nagaland Congress turns to voters for 2028 roadmap
description: The party plans statewide outreach involving churches, village bodies, civil society groups & ordinary citizens alike.
content:
File image of Nagaland Congress' Working President Khriedi Theunuo. (Photo:INC Nagaland/Meta/X)File image of Nagaland Congress' Working President Khriedi Theunuo. (Photo:INC Nagaland/Meta/X)Kohima, Jun 13: The Nagaland unit of the Congress has launched "People's Voice Questionnaire", a statewide exercise seeking feedback from the people to formulate its vision and policies for the 2028 Assembly elections.
Addressing a press conference on Saturday, the Nagaland Congress' Working President Khriedi Theunuo said the questionnaire has been designed to gather opinions on key issues affecting the state and to better understand the aspirations, concerns and priorities of the people. 
He said the feedback would assist the Congress in developing a people-centric policy framework and election agenda. 
"The Congress wants to know what the people expect from the party and what kind of future they envision for Nagaland. The responses will help shape our policy-making and preparations for the 2028 elections," Theunuo said. 
According to him, the questionnaires have already been distributed to all the district units of the party. 
The party plans to reach a wide cross-section of society, including church leaders, village authorities, social activists, community organisations and citizens from different walks of life, he said. 
Stating that "a strong democracy begins when people speak honestly", Theunuo said the exercise aims to give citizens an opportunity to directly influence the party's future direction. 
Nagaland Congress chief and the state's lone Lok Sabha MP S Supongmeren Jamir said the initiative comes at a time when the Congress is attempting to rebuild itself in the state after more than two decades out of power. 
"Until the voters express their views and aspirations, no government can formulate policies that truly reflect the needs of the people," he said. 
Acknowledging the party's electoral setbacks, Jamir said the Congress was prepared to examine its weaknesses and reconnect with grassroots voters. 
PTI

title: Man stabbed in Mumbai local over seat dispute; two held
content:
Representational ImageRepresentational ImageMumbai, June 13:� Two persons have been arrested, and a search is underway for a third accused after a 26-year-old man was allegedly assaulted and stabbed following a dispute over a seat in a Mumbai local train, the Government Railway Police (GRP) said on Saturday.
According to Mumbai GRP, the incident took place on Thursday morning aboard a local train travelling from Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Terminus (CSMT) to Khopoli.
The victim has been identified as Satish Pathak (26), a resident of Mumbai’s Masjid Bunder area. He sustained serious injuries in the knife attack and is currently undergoing treatment.
A Mumbai GRP official said the altercation began after an argument broke out between Pathak and a group of youths over seating in the luggage compartment of the train.
�“The train had crossed Wangani railway station when a dispute started between the victim and some youths over sitting on a seat in the luggage compartment. The argument soon escalated into a physical fight,” the official said.
According to investigators, the accused allegedly assaulted Pathak and beat him severely. During the scuffle, one of the attackers allegedly stabbed him in the stomach, leaving him critically injured.
Following the incident, Karjat GRP launched an investigation and tracked down the suspects.
Acting on a tip-off, Karjat GRP arrested Imran Idris Ansari (18) and Karan Kanhaiyalal Agrawal (22) within 12 hours of the incident, according to the Mumbai GRP official.
Police said efforts are continuing to trace and arrest a third accused who is believed to have been involved in the attack.
The swift arrests were made after investigators gathered information from witnesses and conducted searches in the area. Officials said the accused are being questioned, and further investigation is underway to establish the exact sequence of events and the role of each individual involved.
The GRP has registered a case in connection with the incident and said further legal action will be taken based on the findings of the investigation.
 
--IANS

title: Indian hurdler Tejas Shirse achieves CWG qualifying standard
content:
File image of veteran hurdler Tejas Shirse(Photo: @India_AllSports/X)File image of veteran hurdler Tejas Shirse(Photo: @India_AllSports/X)Ludhiana, June 1:� Indian hurdler Tejas Shirse delivered a landmark performance at the Indian Athletics Series 9 on Saturday, clocking a stunning 13.27 seconds in the men's 110m hurdles to set a new national record and strengthen his case for a place in the 2026 Commonwealth Games squad.
The 24-year-old athlete eclipsed his own national record of 13.41 seconds set in 2024, producing one of the finest sprint-hurdle performances by an Indian athlete. His effort also comfortably surpassed the Athletics Federation of India's Commonwealth Games qualification standard of 13.39 seconds.
Running in the B final, Shirse exploded out of the blocks and established an early lead. Despite clipping the final hurdle, he maintained his momentum to the finish line, stopping the clock at 13.27 seconds. Krishik M finished second in 13.55 seconds but was never able to close the gap on the record-breaking hurdler.
The timing represents a significant leap from Shirse's gold medal-winning run of 13.50 seconds at last month's Federation Cup in Ranchi and places him among Asia's leading performers this season. His 13.27-second effort currently ranks sixth on the continental list for 2026.
The achievement carries additional significance for Indian athletics. If selected, Shirse could become the first Indian male hurdler to compete at the Commonwealth Games since Siddharth Thingalaya's appearance in 2014.
Elsewhere, national record holder Mohammad Afsal P endured a disappointing outing in the men's 800m, clocking 1:47.00 and falling short of the 1:45.00 qualification standard, a result that may end his hopes of making the Commonwealth Games team.
Attention later shifted to the men's javelin throw competition, where several athletes were chasing qualification marks. With World Championships finalist Sachin Yadav sidelined by an elbow injury and double Olympic medallist Neeraj Chopra continuing his recovery from injury, throwers including Rohit Yadav, Shivam Lohakare and Kishore Kumar Jena remained in contention for a place in the national squad.
 
 
--IANS

title: Assam sees one of India's sharpest inflation jumps, over 2% in 2026: Report
description: The findings come against the backdrop of India's overall Consumer Price Index inflation rising to 3.93% in May
content:
File image of a vegetable market in Guwahati (Photo: AT)File image of a vegetable market in Guwahati (Photo: AT)New Delhi, June 13: Assam has recorded one of the steepest increases in consumer inflation among major Indian states this year, with the state registering a rise of more than 2% during January-May 2026, far outpacing the national average increase of 1.19% over the same period, according to an SBI Research report.
Along with Andhra Pradesh and Odisha, Assam is among only three major states to have seen such a sharp acceleration in inflation this year, the report noted. 
"The distribution of inflation among states and UTs highlights that the majority of states' inflation falls between 2 and 4 per cent during January-May 2026," the report said. 
The findings come against the backdrop of India's overall Consumer Price Index inflation rising to 3.93% in May, up from 3.48% in April, driven by higher fuel prices and a slight LPG shortage. 
The SBI Research report highlights a broad regional disparity in inflation across the country. Of the 36 states and Union Territories, between 12 and 14 are recording inflation above the national level. 
Several major states including Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Madhya Pradesh and Rajasthan continue to see inflation higher than the all-India average. 
Telangana remains the worst-affected, topping the inflation chart among all states since January 2026 with a rate of 6.15% in May. The report attributes this to higher fuel taxes, rising rural wages and dependence on imported fresh produce. 
Fuel and cooking gas emerge as the primary drivers of the May inflation uptick nationally. 
Transport inflation surged by 176 basis points to 1.8% in May compared to April, while inflation in restaurants and accommodation services, heavily dependent on LPG, rose by 154 basis points to 5.7%. 
Precious metals also added to the pressure. Silver jewellery contributed 56 basis points to headline inflation, followed by gold at 30 basis points. 
The moderate rise in gold and silver prices drove an increase of nearly 80 basis points in the personal care category. LPG shortages also pushed up prices of cooked meat and cooked snacks, the report added. 
IANS

title: How to watch India vs Pakistan Women's T20 WC match live
content:
Photo: IANSPhoto: IANSBirmingham, June 13:� The spotlight will firmly be on Edgbaston when India and Pakistan face off in their campaign opener of the ICC Women's T20 World Cup 2026 on Sunday. Few fixtures in international cricket generate as much interest as an India-Pakistan encounter, and both teams will be eager to set the tone for their World Cup campaigns with an early victory.
The Women in Blue arrive with a settled and experienced squad under Harmanpreet Kaur. Their batting unit remains one of the strongest in the tournament, led by the aggressive opening combination of Smriti Mandhana and Shafali Verma. The pair's ability to dominate the powerplay could place Pakistan under immediate pressure.
The Women in Green, meanwhile, begin a new chapter under captain Fatima Sana. With a young squad looking to establish itself on the global stage, Sunday's contest presents an opportunity to challenge one of the tournament favourites.
The bowling battle could prove decisive. Pakistan's hopes will largely rest on experienced spinners Sadia Iqbal and Nida Dar, who must find ways to contain India's deep batting resources on a venue known for rewarding strokeplay. India's bowling attack appears equally well equipped for the conditions. Renuka Singh's movement with the new ball and Deepti Sharma's control through the middle overs provide valuable variety and experience.
Although historical results favour India, rivalry matches often produce unpredictable outcomes. The occasion, the crowd and the pressure of a World Cup opener can create a contest very different from what form books suggest. With both teams desperate to begin their campaigns positively, fans can expect a fiercely contested encounter in Birmingham.
When: Sunday, June 14, 7:00 PM IST
Where: Edgbaston, Birmingham
Where to watch: The IND vs PAK match will be broadcast on Star Sports channels, with JioHotstar live-streaming the game.
Squads:
India: Harmanpreet Kaur (c), Smriti Mandhana, Shafali Verma, Jemimah Rodrigues, Bharti Fulmali, Deepti Sharma, Richa Ghosh, Shree Charani, Yastika Bhatia, Nandani Sharma, Arundhati Reddy, Renuka Singh, Kranti Gaud, Shreyanka Patil, Radha Yadav
Pakistan: Fatima Sana (c), Gull Feroza, Ayesha Zafar, Iram Javed, Eyman Fatima, Aliya Riaz, Natalia Parvaiz, Saira Jabeen, Muneeba Ali, Tuba Hassan, Rameen Shamim, Sadia Iqbal, Nashra Sandhu, Diana Baig, Tasmia Rubab
 
 
--IANS

title: Five IAF personnel dead as AN-32 crashes at Jorhat's Rowriah air base
description: Paradrop sortie turned fatal as aircraft requested landing, veered off runway and caught fire, sources said
content:
The deceased have been identified as Sqn Ldr Prashant Singh, Flt Lt Shubham Kumar, Sgt Jitendra Sharma, Agniveervayu Khemaram Kumawat and Agniveervayu Danish AlamThe deceased have been identified as Sqn Ldr Prashant Singh, Flt Lt Shubham Kumar, Sgt Jitendra Sharma, Agniveervayu Khemaram Kumawat and Agniveervayu Danish AlamJorhat, June 13: Five Indian Air Force personnel were killed on Saturday morning after an AN-32 transport aircraft crashed at Rowriah Air Force Station in Jorhat, triggering panic among residents in the surrounding areas.
The deceased have been identified as Squadron Leader Prashant Singh, Flight Lieutenant Shubham Kumar, Sergeant Jitendra Sharma, Agniveervayu Khemaram Kumawat and Agniveervayu Danish Alam. 
The Indian Air Force extended its condolences to the bereaved families. "IAF extends its deepest condolences to the bereaved families and stands firmly with them in this hour of grief," it said in a social media post.

 
 
  
 

Rescue team at the site of the crash. (AT Photo)
According to sources, the aircraft took off at around 9:45 am from Jorhat to Chabua to conduct a paradrop sortie.
Shortly after take-off, it reportedly requested a landing. During the approach, the aircraft veered off the runway, crossed the parallel taxiway and broke into two pieces before catching fire. Rescue efforts were launched immediately. 
In an earlier post, the IAF confirmed the accident occurred during a routine sortie in Jorhat, adding that crash site management and initial enquiries were under way. It urged the public to refrain from speculation until preliminary findings were available. 
Thick plumes of black and white smoke were seen billowing from the crash site. 
A local resident who witnessed the incident described the scene. "Suddenly, we saw thick black and white smoke rising from the area. We also heard a loud explosion-like sound, unlike anything we had heard before. It created panic among people in the vicinity," he said.

 
 
  
 

Thick plumes of smoke billowing from the crash site. (AT Photo)
Expressing grief over the deaths of the Indian Air Force personnel, Chief Minister Himanta Biswa Sarma described the loss as deeply unfortunate.
"Pained by the loss of five brave Air Warriors of @IAF_MCC in the AN-32 accident in Jorhat. On behalf of the people of Assam, I offer my thoughts and prayers to the bereaved families in this hour of grief," Sarma said in a social media post.

 
  
   
    Pained by the loss of 5 brave Air Warriors of @IAF_MCC in the AN 32 accident in Jorhat.The loss of Sqn Ldr Prashant Singh, Flt Lt Shubham Kumar, Sgt Jitendra Sharma, Agniveervayu Khemaram Kumawat and Agniveervayu Danish Alam in the line of duty is deeply unfortunate.On behalf… https://t.co/l2eP4jVd4B— Himanta Biswa Sarma (@himantabiswa) June 13, 2026
    
  
 

He added that the Assam government is in touch with the concerned authorities and stands ready to extend all necessary support and assistance.
The Soviet-origin AN-32 is a twin-engine tactical transport aircraft that has served as one of the IAF's key workhorses for decades, particularly in challenging terrains and high-altitude sectors across the Northeast and along the Himalayan frontier. The aircraft was first acquired in 1984. 
Saturday's crash is the third major accident involving the AN-32 in the last ten years, and the deadliest stretch in the type's operational history in India. At least 42 IAF personnel have been killed in AN-32 crashes since 2016. 
On July 22, 2016, an AN-32 went missing over the Bay of Bengal while flying from Tambaram Air Force Station in Chennai to Port Blair, killing all 29 people on board. 
On June 3, 2019, another aircraft of the same type crashed in hilly terrain while heading to Mechuka in Arunachal Pradesh's West Siang district, near the border with China, killing all 13 on board.
With inputs from agencies

title: Bangladesh spinner Nayeem Hasan claims police harassment in Chattogram
content:
File image of Bangladesh spinner Nayeem(Photo: @arnuX05/X)File image of Bangladesh spinner Nayeem(Photo: @arnuX05/X)Chattogram, June 13:� Bangladesh Test off-spinner Nayeem Hasan has alleged that he was harassed and assaulted by members of a law enforcement agency in his hometown of Chattogram on Saturday night, with local police authorities promising a full investigation into the incident.
After his release from police custody Nayeem, who was recently included in Bangladesh's squad for their impending Test series against Zimbabwe, was in a visibly agitated state as he described the night's events.
It is alleged that the incident occurred late last night after Nayeem returned home after featuring for the last time in Prime Bank Cricket Club's final Premier League, Bangladesh's top List A competition game.
"My Premier League game was going on, my flight was delayed. I was coming from the airport through my CNG and at around 11:25 PM they (Police) stopped my CNG under the Lankan Bazar flyover and took the documents from the driver," Nayeem was quoted by Cricbuzz as saying.
According to the off-spinner he was initially cooperative with the authorities, even offering them to examine his bags.
"I told the police, 'If necessary, you can check my bag' but they grabbed me by the throat and said 'Get in the car' and took me into their car," he said.
Nayeem then claims that things quickly escalated as he tried to get to the bottom of what was happening.
"I said 'Why are you grabbing me by the throat' but they pushed me out. Then they grabbed me, beat me and abused me," he added.
The 25-year-old goes on to suggest that his status as a national cricketer made little difference at that initial stage of the ordeal.
"I even told them that I am a national cricketer and represent the country in Test matches but they hardly bothered to listen and only when they got phone calls and were informed about me, did they change their attitude," he said.
The claims have drawn attention in Bangladesh's cricket circles, especially with Nayeem now in line to represent his country in the forthcoming Test series against Zimbabwe.
Addressing the claims, Amirul Islam, Deputy Police Commissioner (North) of the Chattogram Metropolitan Police assured that a proper inquiry was on and that action would be taken if any police officers are found guilty.
"We can assure you that he (Nayeem) will get justice," Amirul told reporters.
"We are investigating the matter and whoever did it will be punished because we have zero tolerance in this regard considering police have a protocol and they must maintain that because our image is involved with it," he added.
 
--IANS

title: Assam bars Aadhaar for over-18 applicants, cites illegal immigration concerns
description: Satellite city near Guwahati Airport and Rs 2,000 cr rural livelihood scheme also get Cabinet nod
content:
CM Sarma chairs the third cabinet meeting of his new government, on Saturday. (Photo:@CMOfficeAssam/X)CM Sarma chairs the third cabinet meeting of his new government, on Saturday. (Photo:@CMOfficeAssam/X)Guwahati, June 13:�The Assam Cabinet, on Saturday, approved a series of measures aimed at tightening safeguards against illegal immigration, generating rural employment and planning future urban expansion, including restrictions on Aadhaar issuance for adults, the creation of a satellite city near Guwahati Airport and the rollout of a new livelihood scheme.
Announcing the decisions after the third Cabinet meeting since the formation of the new government, Chief Minister Himanta Biswa Sarma said Aadhaar cards would no longer be issued in Assam to individuals above the age of 18, except under special circumstances. 
"From now on, people above 18 years of age will not be issued Aadhaar cards in Assam. We have already achieved near-total Aadhaar saturation in the state. This decision has been taken to ensure that illegal Bangladeshi immigrants are unable to obtain Aadhaar cards," Sarma told reporters. 
He said Aadhaar enrolment in some districts had already exceeded 100%, raising concerns about potential misuse of the system. 
According to the new policy, any Aadhaar enrolment for individuals above 18 years of age will require prior approval from the State Government, with District Commissioners required to seek permission before processing such applications. 
The government, however, has granted a temporary exemption to eligible members of Scheduled Tribe communities and tea garden workers who are yet to receive Aadhaar cards. 
Sarma said Aadhaar enrolment for these groups would continue until March 2027. From April 1, 2027, the same restrictions would apply to them unless special permission is obtained from the State Government. 
The Chief Minister clarified that the restrictions would not apply to individuals below 18 years of age. 
Satellite city near airport 
In another major decision, the Cabinet approved the establishment of a satellite city near Lokpriya Gopinath Bordoloi International Airport and constituted the Guwahati Satellite City Development Authority to oversee the project. 
The proposed satellite city will be developed in areas behind the airport, covering parts of Palasbari, Sualkuchi and adjoining regions near the bridge. The area will fall under the jurisdiction of the Guwahati Metropolitan Development Authority (GMDA). 
Rs 2,000 crore rural scheme from July 1 
The Cabinet also cleared the implementation of the Viksit Bharat–Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission (VB-G RAM G) in Assam from July 1, with an allocation of Rs 2,000 crore for the current financial year. 
Under the scheme, each panchayat will receive financial assistance ranging from Rs 70 lakh to Rs 1.5 crore, depending on local requirements. The initiative aims to generate employment, create community assets and strengthen livelihood opportunities in rural areas. 
To ensure transparency and prevent irregularities, the Cabinet approved the formation of a monitoring committee to oversee implementation. 
Sarma said the government would closely monitor the scheme from the outset and address shortcomings that had affected previous employment programmes such as MGNREGA. 
Cabinet to vet IAS deputations 
In a separate administrative reform, the Cabinet decided that the issuance of No Objection Certificates (NOCs) for Central and inter-state deputation of All India Service officers, including IAS officers, will now require Cabinet approval. 
The Chief Minister also indicated that a high-level dignitary may visit Assam between July 1 and July 3 along with Prime Minister Narendra Modi, although the programme is yet to be formally confirmed. 
Preparations for the possible visit will begin immediately, Sarma said, while seeking the cooperation of residents, particularly in Guwahati. 
He further announced that the Assam Legislative Assembly's Budget session, scheduled to begin on July 6, is expected to continue for more than 21 days, during which the full Budget for 2026-27 will be presented.
